events.title: {surname} Management, Brooklyn Office
events.scene: Fresh out of Wharton, you report for duty at your father Fred's company, collecting rents in Brooklyn and Queens. The buildings are middle-class apartment complexes, profitable but unglamorous. Fred hands you a clipboard and a list of late payers. He expects results, not excuses.
events.researchNote: After graduating from the Wharton School in 1968, Donald Trump joined his father Fred Trump's real estate firm, Trump Management, which operated middle-income rental housing in Brooklyn, Queens, and Staten Island.

choices.text: Shadow Fred, learn every angle of the rent business.
choices.outcome: You absorb Fred's hard-nosed playbook. The old man nods, rarely, but he nods. The lessons stick.

choices.text: Tell Fred outer-borough rentals are beneath you.
choices.outcome: Fred frowns. The kid wants Manhattan, not Coney Island. Ambition noted, patience not.

choices.text: Make an example of the late payers, fast and hard.
choices.outcome: Rents come in on time now. Tenants learn the name Trump means no extensions. Fred is quietly pleased.

events.title: United States v. {surname} Management
events.scene: The Justice Department sues you and Fred for refusing to rent to Black applicants across your apartment complexes. The case is front-page embarrassment. A young, ferocious attorney named Roy Cohn tells you the move is not to settle quietly but to countersue the government for one hundred million dollars and never, ever apologize.
events.researchNote: In 1973 the U.S. Department of Justice sued Trump Management for racial discrimination under the Fair Housing Act; on Roy Cohn's advice, Trump countersued the government for $100 million and the case ended in a 1975 consent decree admitting no wrongdoing.

choices.text: Hire Roy Cohn and countersue the government.
choices.outcome: Cohn becomes your dark mentor. Attack, deny, countersue, never settle on your knees. The doctrine will define you for decades.

choices.text: Settle quietly and change the rental policy.
choices.outcome: The lawyers paper it over, the policy changes, the headlines fade. Cohn shrugs and finds another client. You took the soft road, and without his ferocity the government keeps digging into Fred's old practices.

choices.text: Pin the policy on Fred and the old management.
choices.outcome: You distance yourself from the old man's practices in public. Fred says nothing, but you have left him alone to absorb the government's case. The family ledger remembers.

events.title: The Reckoning Lands on Fred
events.scene: The discrimination case metastasizes. Investigators pry open {surname} Management's books and find the FHA-era pattern that built the rental fortune in the first place: doormen coding Black applicants' files with a 'C,' superintendents quoting phantom vacancies. Prosecutors now talk not of a consent decree but of indicting Fred himself, freezing the cash flow that quietly underwrites your every leap. If the old man falls, the floor beneath you falls with him.
events.researchNote: The 1973 U.S. v. Trump Management suit documented practices like marking Black applicants' applications with a 'C' for 'colored' and falsely telling them no units were available; Fred Trump's outer-borough rental empire was the seed capital that financed Donald's later ventures, so a catastrophic legal outcome would have removed the foundation of the entire empire.

choices.text: Stay clear and let Fred face it alone.
choices.outcome: You keep your distance and the case devours the old man. Fred is indicted, the rental fortune bled white by fines and shame. The seed capital is gone, and with it the only ladder you ever had to Manhattan.

choices.text: Wrest control of {surname} Management to save the company.
choices.outcome: You shove the old man aside, take the title, and rebuild the firm's defense around yourself. Fred is indicted but the company survives in your hands. The seed capital is salvaged, scorched but intact, and the rise continues.

choices.text: Belatedly hire Roy Cohn to counterattack the government.
choices.outcome: You find Cohn late but in time. He floods the case with countersuits and venom until the government settles for a face-saving decree. Fred's name survives, the fortune holds, and you have taken the reins from the inside.

events.title: Settle for Enough
events.scene: The rent rolls come in like clockwork. Fred's middle-class buildings in Brooklyn and Queens throw off a steady, unglamorous fortune, and Ivana wants a real home, real children, a life. Manhattan glitters across the river, but the climb means debt, lawyers, and never being home. You could simply run what your father built, well and quietly, and call it enough.
events.researchNote: Fred Trump's outer-borough rental business was genuinely profitable and stable; Donald's pivot to high-leverage Manhattan and Atlantic City ventures was a choice to chase prestige and scale rather than a financial necessity, making a contented, lower-ambition succession a plausible alternate life.

choices.text: Run the rental business well and build a quiet life.
choices.outcome: You settle into the work your father knew: solid buildings, paid-up tenants, a marriage that lasts and children who know your face. No skyline carries your name, and you find you do not miss it.

choices.text: Grow the rentals modestly, no Manhattan, no debt.
choices.outcome: You add a few buildings on the same cautious model Fred taught you, no banks circling, no headlines. Wealthy but not insanely so, you become the steady steward of a real, durable business.

choices.text: It is not enough. Set your eyes across the river.
choices.outcome: The rent rolls feel like a cage. You turn your back on 'enough' and point yourself at Manhattan, where the debt is bigger and so is the name. Ivana sees the life she wanted recede.

events.title: Steward of the Outer Boroughs
events.scene: Years pass and the contented path holds. You sit atop a clean, debt-free rental empire spanning Brooklyn, Queens, and Staten Island, the same machine Fred built and you kept humming. Bankers court you for grander schemes; reporters do not call at all. A son is old enough now to learn the rent business, just as you once did from a clipboard and a list of late payers.
events.researchNote: Fred Trump ran Trump Management as a stable, family-succession business for decades; a Donald who stayed in that model would have remained a wealthy but obscure outer-borough landlord, eventually handing the firm to the next generation rather than pursuing celebrity and Manhattan towers.

choices.text: Groom your son to inherit the firm, just as Fred did.
choices.outcome: You hand the clipboard to the next Trump and watch him learn the trade. The cycle closes the way it began: quiet, solvent, and entirely out of the headlines. This life simply, peaceably, holds.

choices.text: Keep the books clean and the ambitions small.
choices.outcome: No towers, no casinos, no junk bonds. Just a steady fortune and a steady home. The men who chased the skyline will be famous and ruined; you will be neither.

choices.text: Finally take a banker's call about a Manhattan deal.
choices.outcome: One phone call and the quiet life cracks open. You let Manhattan back in, and the contented steward dissolves into the man who always wanted more. The old peace will not return.

events.title: Cohn's Finishing School
events.scene: Roy Cohn takes you to dinner at his townhouse, the walls thick with photos of senators, mobsters, and cardinals. He teaches you his three rules: never apologize, always attack, and claim victory whatever the outcome. He has no contract and demands no fee up front; loyalty is the only currency he accepts.
events.researchNote: Roy Cohn, formerly chief counsel to Senator Joseph McCarthy, mentored Trump throughout the 1970s and 1980s, instilling his combative tactics of relentless counterattack, denial, and claiming victory regardless of facts.

choices.text: Take the three rules as gospel.
choices.outcome: Cohn's rules become your reflexes. You will never again be caught flat-footed by a critic, a regulator, or a reporter.

choices.text: Use Cohn but keep your own counsel.
choices.outcome: You take what serves you and leave the rest. Cohn notices the hedge, but the connections are still gold.

events.title: Crossing the River
events.scene: You rent a small apartment in Manhattan and start prowling the Upper East Side clubs, trading on the family name and a borrowed credit line. The real money, you decide, is across the East River from where Fred built his fortune. You want your name on a skyline, not a rent roll.
events.researchNote: In the early 1970s Trump moved to Manhattan and joined exclusive clubs like Le Club, cultivating connections among the city's social and business elite as he pivoted toward Manhattan real estate.

choices.text: Charm the city's power brokers at Le Club.
choices.outcome: You collect contacts like poker chips. Bankers, lawyers, society columnists, all now know the young Trump.

choices.text: Stay close to Fred's books and don't overspend.
choices.outcome: Cautious, solvent, and unknown. The skyline waits for someone louder.

events.title: Ford to City: Drop Dead
events.scene: New York is days from bankruptcy. President Ford refuses a federal bailout and the Daily News screams FORD TO CITY: DROP DEAD across its front page. Every banker you know is fleeing Manhattan real estate. For a contrarian with nerve, a dying city is a fire sale.
events.researchNote: In 1975 New York City teetered on bankruptcy; President Gerald Ford initially refused federal aid, prompting the Daily News headline 'FORD TO CITY: DROP DEAD.' The crisis depressed Manhattan property values, creating openings for bold investors.

choices.text: Buy options on distressed sites while everyone runs.
choices.outcome: You tie up rights to crumbling lots for pennies. When the city claws back to life, you will be holding the deeds nobody else wanted.

choices.text: Hoard cash and wait for the bottom.
choices.outcome: You sit on your wallet while braver men plant flags. Prudent, but the legends are made by those who buy when the city is on fire.

choices.text: Lobby Albany for tax breaks to rescue developers.
choices.outcome: You learn the dark art of squeezing public subsidy from a broke government. The relationships in Albany will pay off soon.

events.title: The Commodore Gamble
events.scene: The Commodore Hotel beside Grand Central is a decaying wreck and the city is nearly bankrupt. You propose buying it, gutting it, and reopening it as a gleaming Grand Hyatt, but only if the city grants you an unprecedented 40-year property-tax abatement. Everyone says it can't be done. Fred's lessons whisper in your ear.
events.researchNote: Trump's breakout deal was the 1976 acquisition and redevelopment of the run-down Commodore Hotel into the Grand Hyatt, secured with a controversial 40-year tax abatement from a near-bankrupt New York City, opening in 1980.

choices.text: Pressure City Hall until the tax abatement is yours.
choices.outcome: The Grand Hyatt rises in chrome and glass. The unprecedented abatement makes you, at 30, a Manhattan player overnight.

choices.text: Bring in Hyatt as a partner to share the risk.
choices.outcome: Splitting the upside steadies the deal but dilutes the legend. Still, the Commodore becomes your launchpad.

choices.text: Too risky in a broke city. Pass on it.
choices.outcome: The wreck stays a wreck for now. Someone bolder would have seen the skyline in the rubble.

events.title: The Oil Shock
events.scene: OPEC's embargo sends gas lines around the block and prices through the roof. Stagflation grips the country: inflation and stagnation at once, a beast the economists swore was impossible. Construction costs spike and credit tightens. Every dollar you borrow now costs more next year.
events.researchNote: The 1973 OPEC oil embargo quadrupled crude prices and triggered years of 'stagflation' — simultaneous high inflation and economic stagnation — that battered the U.S. economy and the real estate and construction sectors through the 1970s.

choices.text: Lock in long-term financing before rates climb further.
choices.outcome: You bolt down cheap money while you can. When inflation chews up the dollar, your fixed payments shrink in real terms.

choices.text: Bet that inflation will float all your assets higher.
choices.outcome: You ride the inflation wave, watching paper values balloon. It feels like genius until the bill for the borrowing arrives.

choices.text: Cut spending and wait for the storm to pass.
choices.outcome: You batten the hatches and preserve cash. Survivable, unglamorous, and forgotten by the press.

events.title: The Czech Model
events.scene: You meet Ivana Zelnickova, a Czech-born skier and fashion model, at a New York restaurant during the Montreal Olympics buzz. She is sharp, glamorous, and ambitious, the perfect partner for a man building an empire. The wedding is splashed across the society pages.
events.researchNote: Trump married Ivana Zelnickova, a Czech model and former competitive skier, in April 1977; she later played a prominent role managing the Plaza Hotel and the Atlantic City casino operations.

choices.text: Build a glamorous power-couple brand together.
choices.outcome: Ivana becomes a fixture of the empire and the gossip columns. Together you are New York royalty in the making.

choices.text: Marry her but keep her out of the business.
choices.outcome: A handsome marriage, a quieter partnership. The columns get less to chew on.

choices.text: Make her sign an ironclad prenuptial agreement.
choices.outcome: The marriage starts with a contract built to protect your fortune. Cohn drafts the terms. Romance has rarely been so well-collateralized.

events.title: Feeding the Tabloids
events.scene: A reporter calls asking about your net worth. You could give a sober figure, or you could inflate it wildly and watch the headlines bloom. The New York press is hungry, and you are learning that perception is leverage. Every quote is a brick in the brand.
events.researchNote: Trump cultivated a brash public image in the late 1970s and 1980s, frequently exaggerating his wealth to reporters and even posing as a fictional spokesman named 'John Barron' to plant favorable stories with the press.

choices.text: Triple the number and let the headlines run.
choices.outcome: The press eats it up. The Trump legend grows faster than the actual balance sheet, and that is the point.

choices.text: Phone reporters yourself as 'John Barron,' your own spokesman.
choices.outcome: You become your own publicist, planting flattering stories under a fake name. Brazen, effective, and one day deeply embarrassing.

choices.text: Give the honest, modest figure.
choices.outcome: Accurate and forgettable. The headline runs on page nine, if at all.

events.title: The Night the Lights Went Out
events.scene: A lightning strike plunges the entire city into darkness. Looting and arson sweep the boroughs through a sweltering July night. By morning, thousands of stores are gutted and the image of a city in chaos flashes across the nation. New York looks ungovernable, and that scares away exactly the money you covet.
events.researchNote: The July 1977 New York City blackout, caused by lightning strikes on power lines, plunged the city into darkness for up to 25 hours, triggering widespread looting and arson amid the era's fiscal crisis and urban decay.

choices.text: Publicly call for law and order and tougher policing.
choices.outcome: You position yourself as the voice of a frightened city. The law-and-order pose plays well and previews a future political persona.

choices.text: Quietly buy up fire-damaged blocks at distress prices.
choices.outcome: While the embers cool, you make offers. The city's misery becomes your inventory, bought for cents on the dollar.

choices.text: Keep your head down until the city recovers.
choices.outcome: You wait out the chaos behind closed doors. Safe, but the bolder operators are already circling the rubble.

events.title: The Hollowed City
events.scene: The middle class is fleeing for the suburbs, taking the tax base with it. Whole neighborhoods stand abandoned, landlords torching buildings for insurance money in the Bronx. The city you bet on is hollowing out. Yet empty land and desperate sellers are the raw material of empire.
events.researchNote: 1970s New York suffered severe urban decay and 'white flight' to the suburbs, with arson-for-insurance plaguing areas like the South Bronx; the depopulation and collapsing values reshaped the city's real estate landscape.

choices.text: Bet the whole brand on a Manhattan comeback.
choices.outcome: You stake everything on the idea that Manhattan will rise from its ashes gleaming. The contrarian bet of a lifetime.

choices.text: Squeeze the outer-borough rentals while values fall.
choices.outcome: You wring every dollar from Fred's old buildings as tenants struggle. Profitable, hard, and exactly the reputation you are cultivating.

events.title: The Bonwit Teller Friezes
events.scene: To build Trump Tower on Fifth Avenue, you must demolish the old Bonwit Teller department store. The Metropolitan Museum wants its prized Art Deco limestone friezes preserved. Saving them means delays and crane costs; jackhammering them means schedule and savings. The press is watching.
events.researchNote: During the 1980 demolition for Trump Tower, Trump ordered the destruction of Bonwit Teller's prized Art Deco bas-relief sculptures rather than donate them to the Metropolitan Museum of Art as promised, citing cost and delay, drawing widespread criticism.

choices.text: Jackhammer them. Schedule beats sentiment.
choices.outcome: The friezes crumble to dust and the city howls. But Trump Tower rises on schedule, a gold-and-glass monument to your name.

choices.text: Eat the cost and donate them to the Met.
choices.outcome: The Met gets its treasures and you get rare good press. Trump Tower opens a little later, a little classier.

choices.text: Destroy them but blame anonymous workers in the press.
choices.outcome: You deny ordering it and pin the demolition on faceless contractors. Cohn would be proud. The friezes are gone either way.

events.title: Gold on Fifth Avenue
events.scene: Trump Tower opens its doors: a 58-story tower of bronze glass with a pink-marble atrium, a five-story waterfall, and your name in gold over the entrance. Celebrities and money pour in. This is the building that makes the Trump name a luxury brand. How do you market it?
events.researchNote: Trump Tower opened in 1983 on Fifth Avenue, featuring a 60-foot indoor waterfall and pink Breccia Pernice marble atrium; it became Trump's flagship and the headquarters of his organization.

choices.text: Position the Trump name as pure luxury.
choices.outcome: The name TRUMP now means gold, glass, and excess. Buyers pay a premium just for the letters over the door.

choices.text: Fill it fast, screen tenants loosely.
choices.outcome: The units fill quickly with cash buyers, some of them shadowy. The money is fast; the questions come later.

events.title: Morning in America
events.scene: Ronald Reagan takes the White House preaching tax cuts, deregulation, and unapologetic wealth. The Economic Recovery Tax Act slashes top rates and showers real estate with depreciation breaks. The political winds now blow squarely at your back. A man who builds and brags is suddenly the spirit of the age.
events.researchNote: Reagan's 1981 Economic Recovery Tax Act cut top marginal rates and created generous real estate depreciation rules, fueling a 1980s development boom; the era's celebration of wealth and deregulation aligned closely with Trump's public image.

choices.text: Leverage the new depreciation rules to the hilt.
choices.outcome: You stack project on project, shielded by tax breaks and cheap optimism. The 1980s belong to men who borrow boldly, and you are one of them.

choices.text: Cultivate ties to the new Republican establishment.
choices.outcome: You start showing up at the right dinners with the right donors. The seeds of a political future are quietly planted.

choices.text: Keep building and stay out of politics.
choices.outcome: You let the politicians politick and focus on the cranes. The boom lifts you regardless of which dinners you skip.

events.title: The Boardwalk Beckons
events.scene: New Jersey has legalized gambling and Atlantic City is exploding. You could plant the Trump name on a casino tower on the Boardwalk, financed largely with debt. The gaming commission, the bankers, the bondholders all want a piece. The upside is a glittering casino empire; the downside is leverage that could crush you.
events.researchNote: Trump entered the Atlantic City casino market in the early 1980s, opening Harrah's at Trump Plaza in 1984; he eventually controlled three casinos, heavily financed with debt that later led to bankruptcies.

choices.text: Borrow big and stake your name on the Boardwalk.
choices.outcome: The Trump Plaza casino lights up the Boardwalk. The cash flow is dazzling, the debt mountain is terrifying, and both bear your name.

choices.text: Partner with Harrah's to share the financing.
choices.outcome: You open Harrah's at Trump Plaza with a partner absorbing some of the risk. The name is on the Boardwalk, the debt a bit lighter.

choices.text: Take a cautious minority position instead.
choices.outcome: A modest bet on a hot market. Safer, smaller, and far less likely to make headlines or history.

events.title: Trump's Castle
events.scene: You acquire a second Atlantic City casino, Trump's Castle, and put Ivana in charge of running it. Two Trump casinos now compete on the same Boardwalk, sometimes against each other. Wall Street is lending you anything you ask. The empire grows; so does the gravity of the debt holding it up.
events.researchNote: Trump acquired the Atlantic City property that became Trump's Castle in 1985, with Ivana Trump initially overseeing its operations; the proliferation of Trump casinos created internal competition and mounting, debt-heavy obligations.

choices.text: Borrow even more and run two casinos head to head.
choices.outcome: Two Trump towers of chips, two mountains of junk-bond debt. The cannibalism between them worries the bankers more than it worries you.

choices.text: Hand Ivana real authority over the Castle.
choices.outcome: Ivana proves a tough, capable operator, and the columns love the glamour-couple-running-an-empire angle. For now, the partnership holds.

choices.text: Flip the second casino before the debt compounds.
choices.outcome: You take the quick profit and shed the risk. Prudent, but a smaller legend, and the bankers wonder if you have lost your nerve.

events.title: Dreaming of the Taj
events.scene: Resorts International is in play, and with it the half-finished shell of what could become the largest casino in the world, the Taj Mahal. To win it you will need to issue mountains of high-yield junk bonds at brutal interest rates. Every banker warns it can never throw off enough cash to service the debt. You hear only the word 'largest.'
events.researchNote: In the mid-1980s Trump maneuvered to gain control of the unfinished Taj Mahal casino via the Resorts International takeover battle; the project, eventually opened in 1990, was financed with roughly $675 million in junk bonds at about 14 percent interest and went bankrupt within a year.

choices.text: Chase the Taj with junk bonds, debt be damned.
choices.outcome: You lock up the rights to the biggest casino on Earth, financed at fourteen percent. The grandeur is intoxicating; the math is a slow fuse.

choices.text: Pursue it but fight for survivable financing.
choices.outcome: You wrestle the bankers toward terms you might actually service. Smaller headlines, longer odds of survival.

choices.text: Walk away from the Taj. The numbers don't work.
choices.outcome: You let the white elephant go. The press calls it caution or cowardice. Your balance sheet quietly thanks you.

events.title: The New Jersey Generals
events.scene: You buy the New Jersey Generals of the upstart USFL, a spring football league challenging the mighty NFL. You sign star players, then push the league to move to a fall schedule and sue the NFL for antitrust. It is a high-stakes gamble against the most powerful sports cartel in America.
events.researchNote: Trump purchased the USFL's New Jersey Generals in 1984 and pushed the league to compete directly with the NFL in the fall and pursue an antitrust suit; the USFL won the 1986 lawsuit but was awarded only three dollars in damages and folded.

choices.text: Force the fall schedule and sue the NFL.
choices.outcome: You win the lawsuit and a three-dollar check. The league collapses, but you spent two seasons on the national sports page.

choices.text: Splash cash on a marquee quarterback to steal headlines.
choices.outcome: You buy a star and a season of glory. The Generals are box office; the league's finances are still a sieve.

choices.text: Keep the Generals in their lane in the spring.
choices.outcome: The Generals play steady spring ball. Less drama, less debt, and far fewer cameras pointed your way.

events.title: The West Side Yards
events.scene: You assemble the vast abandoned rail yards along the Hudson, nearly a hundred acres on Manhattan's West Side, and unveil plans for 'Television City,' anchored by the world's tallest building. The community boards, the city, and the press recoil at the scale. To win you will fight zoning battles for years.
events.researchNote: In 1985 Trump bought the roughly 76-acre former Penn Central rail yards on Manhattan's West Side and proposed a massive 'Television City' development including what he billed as the world's tallest building; the megaproject faced years of community and zoning opposition.

choices.text: Demand the world's tallest building and fight City Hall.
choices.outcome: You launch a war over the West Side that will rage for a decade. The renderings dazzle; the zoning trenches are merciless.

choices.text: Negotiate a more modest, approvable plan.
choices.outcome: You trade the world-record fantasy for something that might actually get built. The boards relax; the legend dims a notch.

choices.text: Sit on the land and let it appreciate.
choices.outcome: You bank the acreage and wait, carrying costs nibbling at you. The Hudson view will be worth a fortune to someone, eventually.

events.title: Easy Money from the Thrifts
events.scene: Deregulation has unleashed the savings-and-loan industry, and the thrifts are throwing money at real estate developers with almost no questions asked. The lending is reckless, the appraisals fanciful, and the whole edifice is propped on optimism. For a borrower with grand plans, the spigot has never gushed harder.
events.researchNote: 1980s deregulation of savings-and-loan institutions led to a flood of risky real estate lending and rampant speculation, culminating in the S&L crisis that cost taxpayers over $100 billion when hundreds of thrifts failed by the early 1990s.

choices.text: Tap the thrifts for every dollar they'll lend.
choices.outcome: You drink deep from the deregulated trough, stacking debt on debt. The money is intoxicating; the hangover is being penciled in for the next decade.

choices.text: Borrow, but keep the leverage survivable.
choices.outcome: You take the cheap money but keep a margin of safety. Less spectacular, but you will still be standing when the thrifts collapse.

events.title: Volcker's Vise
events.scene: To strangle inflation, Fed chairman Paul Volcker has driven interest rates to brutal heights, the prime rate above twenty percent. Borrowing is agony and the economy lurches into recession. For a developer who lives on debt, every point of rate is a knife at the throat. But survivors of this squeeze will own the recovery.
events.researchNote: Under Chairman Paul Volcker, the Federal Reserve pushed the prime interest rate above 20 percent in 1980-81 to break double-digit inflation, triggering a severe recession before inflation finally subsided and a long expansion began.

choices.text: Refinance hard and tough out the rate spike.
choices.outcome: You scrape through the worst of Volcker's squeeze, bloodied but solvent. When rates fall, your projects will be poised to soar.

choices.text: Freeze new projects until rates fall.
choices.outcome: You hit pause and conserve. Sensible, but rivals who kept building will own the choicest sites when the boom returns.

choices.text: Borrow more, betting rates will collapse soon.
choices.outcome: You gamble that Volcker will blink and pile on debt at punishing rates. If the timing is wrong, the interest alone could bury you.

events.title: Eyes on the Plaza
events.scene: The legendary Plaza Hotel on Central Park South is the most romantic trophy in New York real estate. Acquiring it would be a vanity purchase more than an investment, and everyone knows it. You famously do not care; you call it a work of art and say you are willing to pay a premium just to own beauty.
events.researchNote: Trump pursued and in 1988 bought the iconic Plaza Hotel for $407.5 million, openly conceding in a New York Times ad that he had not made a financial deal but bought 'a masterpiece — the Mona Lisa,' and installed Ivana as its head.

choices.text: Pursue the Plaza as a pure trophy, price be damned.
choices.outcome: You set your heart on the Mona Lisa of hotels and let the world know you will overpay for art. The headlines swoon; the accountants wince.

choices.text: Admire it but refuse to overpay.
choices.outcome: You walk past the Plaza with your wallet intact. A trophy ungrasped, but the bankers nod with rare approval.

events.title: Black Monday
events.scene: On October 19 the Dow plunges nearly 23 percent in a single day, the worst crash in history. Fortunes evaporate before lunch. Your real-estate empire is debt-heavy and exposed; a sustained credit freeze could turn your leverage into a noose. The decade of easy money may be ending exactly when you are most stretched.
events.researchNote: On 'Black Monday,' October 19, 1987, the Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 22.6 percent in a single session, the largest one-day percentage drop in its history, shaking confidence and foreshadowing tighter credit ahead.

choices.text: Sell assets fast and pay down debt while you can.
choices.outcome: You dump trophies and trim the debt before the credit window slams shut. Painful pride, but the noose loosens around your neck.

choices.text: Call it a buying opportunity and borrow into the panic.
choices.outcome: You play the fearless contrarian on the front page, scooping up the wreckage. The swagger is magnificent; the leverage is now a guillotine.

choices.text: Freeze, hoard cash, and watch the dust settle.
choices.outcome: You stand still amid the carnage and keep your powder dry. Neither hero nor casualty, just a man waiting to see who is left standing.

events.title: The Art of the Deal
events.scene: A publisher wants a book with your name on the cover. A ghostwriter does the heavy lifting, but the persona, the swagger, the deal-making mythology, that is all you. It could become the bible of 1980s ambition and cement you as a national celebrity, or it could expose how much is bluster.
events.researchNote: Trump's 'The Art of the Deal,' co-written with ghostwriter Tony Schwartz, was published in 1987, became a bestseller, and cemented Trump's national image as a master dealmaker and celebrity businessman.

choices.text: Publish it big and become a household name.
choices.outcome: The book tops the bestseller lists for nearly a year. You are no longer just a developer; you are a brand, a guru, an American celebrity.

choices.text: Share the spotlight with your ghostwriter.
choices.outcome: The book still sells, and you earn a quieter kind of respect. The myth grows a touch slower without the full one-man-show treatment.

choices.text: Take sole credit and bury the ghostwriter's name.
choices.outcome: The world believes you wrote every word yourself, the lone genius dealmaker. The ghostwriter seethes in obscurity, and the myth becomes total.

events.title: The Old Man's Safety Net
events.scene: When cash runs tight, Fred is always there: a quiet loan, a co-signature, chips slipped across a casino table to cover a bond payment. The press believes you are a self-made man, and you let them. Privately, the family fortune is the floor beneath every daring leap you take.
events.researchNote: Investigations later revealed Fred Trump propped up Donald's ventures for decades through loans, gifts, and maneuvers like a $3.5 million casino-chip purchase at Trump's Castle in 1990, despite Donald's public self-made image.

choices.text: Lean on Fred's fortune to cover your bolder bets.
choices.outcome: Fred's quiet money keeps the empire afloat through the rough patches. The self-made legend stays intact, propped on the old man's checkbook.

choices.text: Refuse the help to keep the self-made myth pure.
choices.outcome: You wave off the safety net so you can truly call yourself self-made. The myth gleams brighter, the tightrope grows thinner beneath you.

events.title: Wings of Vanity
events.scene: Eastern Air Lines is shedding its Northeast shuttle, and a fleet of jets with TRUMP painted on the fuselage tempts you. It has nothing to do with real estate, but everything to do with plastering your name across America's skies. The financing, of course, would be more debt heaped on an already groaning empire.
events.researchNote: Trump bought Eastern Air Lines' Northeast shuttle in 1988 for $365 million, rebranding it the Trump Shuttle and gilding the planes with luxury fittings; the heavily leveraged airline lost money and was surrendered to creditors by 1992.

choices.text: Buy the shuttle and gild it with your name.
choices.outcome: Gold-plated jets streak across the Northeast with your name on the tail. The vanity is dazzling; the debt is just more straw on the camel's back.

choices.text: Stay out of a business you don't understand.
choices.outcome: You let the planes fly under someone else's name. A vanity denied, but one fewer money-losing trophy bolted to the empire.

events.title: The Mentor's Fall
events.scene: Roy Cohn is dying of AIDS, disbarred for ethics violations, abandoned by the powerful men he once served. He asks you, his prize protege, to stand by him. Loyalty was the one currency he demanded. Now the bill comes due, and the cameras are watching how you treat a fallen patron.
events.researchNote: Roy Cohn was disbarred in 1986 for unethical conduct and died of AIDS-related complications that August; Trump was widely reported to have distanced himself from his ailing former mentor in Cohn's final months.

choices.text: Quietly distance yourself from the dying Cohn.
choices.outcome: You let the dying man drift away, useful no longer. He learned his own lesson too late: loyalty was always a one-way street with you.

choices.text: Stand by him publicly to the end.
choices.outcome: You stay at the bedside of your fallen mentor. A rare display of fidelity that surprises the cynics and, briefly, even yourself.

events.title: A Taste for the Stage
events.scene: Flush with fame from your bestselling book, you take out full-page newspaper ads attacking America's foreign policy and trade deals. Reporters start asking, only half joking, whether you might run for president someday. The roar of national attention is sweeter than any building. The seed of a far larger ambition takes root.
events.researchNote: In September 1987 Trump spent nearly $95,000 on full-page open-letter ads in major newspapers criticizing U.S. foreign and trade policy, fueling speculation about presidential ambitions and marking his first overt step toward national politics.

choices.text: Run the ads and stoke the presidential talk.
choices.outcome: You taste the national stage and find it intoxicating. The buildings were never the endgame. A bigger, stranger ambition is now awake.

choices.text: Wave it off and stay focused on the empire.
choices.outcome: You insist you are just a builder and let the speculation cool. The skyline keeps you busy enough, for now.

events.title: The Drumpf Problem
events.scene: A columnist for the Village Voice has been sniffing around. The Trumps are Drumpfs — Friedrich Drumpf arrived from Bavaria in 1902, ran a hotel chain in the Klondike gold rush, and was deported from Germany before the war for dodging military service. Fred told the family they were Swedish to dodge anti-German feeling after World War II. Now the charade is cracking and your new Manhattan ambitions depend on a clean, American-sounding name.
events.researchNote: Fred Trump told his children — and the press — that the family was Swedish, not German, a fiction maintained for decades to avoid anti-German sentiment in New York's heavily Jewish real estate market. Friedrich Trump (born Drumpf) had emigrated from Bavaria in 1902.

choices.text: Double down on the Swedish story. Grandfather was from Stockholm, full stop.
choices.outcome: The Swedish story holds for now. The Jewish contractors and tenants in Queens relax. The secret festers — a Chekhov's gun chambered for someone else's decade.

choices.text: Lean into it — Germany is engineering and order, not shame.
choices.outcome: The old-money WASP crowd sniffs; some Brooklyn tenants grow cold. But a defiant minority loves the gall of a man who refuses to apologize for his bloodline.

choices.text: Flood the columnist with an announcement about the Commodore. Make him chase the story you want.
choices.outcome: The column never runs. The columnist chases the shinier object. You file away the lesson: a bigger story is always the best suppression.

choices.text: Make personal overtures to prominent Jewish real estate families to build trust regardless.
choices.outcome: You build genuine relationships across ethnic lines in the New York property world. The German question quietly loses its sting — friendships are better armor than fictions.

events.title: Buy When Blood Is in the Streets
events.scene: New York City is technically broke. President Ford has just told the city to drop dead — the Daily News headline writes itself. Midtown skyscrapers are selling at forty cents on the dollar. Half of Manhattan's developers have locked their offices and fled to Miami. Everyone says Manhattan is finished. Fred thinks it's over too. You disagree.
events.researchNote: New York City's 1975 fiscal crisis pushed the city to the brink of default; property values collapsed and buildings were abandoned. Trump's 1974-76 option on the derelict Commodore Hotel beside Grand Central, acquired for a fraction of its pre-crisis value, became the template deal of his rise.

choices.text: Snap up the Commodore Hotel at the distressed price and tie it to a city tax abatement.
choices.outcome: You bet against an entire city's despair, wrap it in a forty-year tax deal, and walk away with a midtown foothold. When New York recovers, the Commodore prints money. The crisis was your ladder.

choices.text: The city could still default. Wait six months for the true bottom.
choices.outcome: You hang back — and lose the Commodore option to a faster hand. But you study every move, and the lesson about crisis-timing will be worth a tuition.

choices.text: Offer to co-develop distressed city-owned buildings as a public-private showcase.
choices.outcome: You position yourself as the savior developer: private money rescuing a broke city. Koch owes you. Albany owes you. The press writes the story you scripted.

choices.text: Fred is right — pull capital back to Queens and ride it out in the outer boroughs.
choices.outcome: You follow Fred back to Brooklyn while Manhattan's bones go on sale. When the city heals, you watch other men become legends on your missed opportunity.

events.title: Stagflation Nation
events.scene: The economy is doing something textbooks say is impossible: prices rising and growth stalling at the same time. The OPEC oil embargo has detonated under every budget. Construction costs are up thirty percent in a year. Your contractors want cost-plus contracts, the banks want floating rates, and the tenants can barely pay the rents that existed before any of this started.
events.researchNote: The 1973-74 OPEC oil embargo and resulting stagflation — simultaneous inflation and stagnation — devastated construction costs and real estate margins across the U.S., forcing developers to renegotiate contracts and financing in real time.

choices.text: Hammer out fixed-price contracts now before costs climb further.
choices.outcome: You lock your contractors in before the next price surge. They hate you for it; the balance sheet thanks you. You learn that contracts are weapons.

choices.text: Hike the rents sharply and blame Washington.
choices.outcome: The rents go up; the protest pickets appear outside the Queens buildings. You become locally infamous and file it as free advertising.

choices.text: Grease Albany to exempt new construction from rent controls.
choices.outcome: The exemption quietly passes in an omnibus bill. You make more on new units than anyone else in the borough, and the politicians get a donor they can count on.

events.title: The Gilded Velvet Rope
events.scene: You have arrived in Manhattan — the Commodore deal is closed, the name is in the papers. And yet the old-money crowd at the Racquet & Tennis Club, the Downtown Association, the Lenox Hill Hospital board — the real estate dynasties who trace their fortunes to the Astors and the Vanderbilts — will not return your calls. A Rockefeller aide lets slip the phrase: 'outer-borough money.' The Gilded Age is over but its ghosts still hold the guest list.
events.researchNote: New York's Gilded-Age dynasties — Astors, Vanderbilts, Whitneys — created a stratum of old-money legitimacy that persisted well into the 1970s and 80s. Developers like Trump who arrived via outer-borough rental housing rather than inherited Manhattan landholdings faced acute social exclusion from established real estate and civic circles.

choices.text: Donate to their charities, attend their galas, and play the long legitimacy game.
choices.outcome: You write the checks, chair the committees, smile at men who privately call you 'the outer-borough builder.' Slowly the doors open. The price is a performance of deference you find corrosive.

choices.text: Go to the tabloids. Call them fossils. Say their 'old money' is just old failure in nicer clothes.
choices.outcome: The Post runs it on page three. The Racquet Club crowd is furious. But every working-class reader in Brooklyn nods along — and remembers the name. You trade their approval for a different, bigger crowd.

choices.text: Let the buildings speak. Put your name on enough skyline and the velvet rope becomes irrelevant.
choices.outcome: You stop chasing their tables and start building their skyline. When Trump Tower opens above Tiffany's, they have to stare at your name every day on their walk to work.

choices.text: Network with the rising Jewish and Italian real estate dynasties who also got shut out.
choices.outcome: You build a coalition of the excluded: the Dursts, the LeFraks, the Rudins who also know the velvet rope from the wrong side. New York's real estate power shifts from lineage to leverage.

events.title: Milken's Money Machine
events.scene: Michael Milken at Drexel Burnham Lambert has invented a new universe: junk bonds, high-yield debt for companies and developers who can't get investment-grade paper. It is the financial rocket fuel of the 1980s and the only reason deals like the Taj Mahal are conceivable. Your banker is on the phone with an offer. The yields are brutal but the capital is infinite — if you have the stomach for it.
events.researchNote: Michael Milken's junk-bond market at Drexel Burnham Lambert transformed 1980s finance, enabling leveraged buyouts and mega-deals that would otherwise have been impossible. Trump's Atlantic City casino empire — particularly the Taj Mahal — was financed substantially through high-yield bonds at rates that made the projects structurally fragile.

choices.text: Take Milken's call and lever up the casino empire on high-yield paper.
choices.outcome: The capital flows in torrents. You are building at a scale that would have required a Vanderbilt fortune a generation ago. The fourteen-percent coupon is a detail — until it isn't.

choices.text: Use high-yield paper for one project but keep the rest conventionally financed.
choices.outcome: You tap Milken for one flagship and keep the rest of the empire in safer hands. Slower build, but the failure of one domino won't topple them all.

choices.text: Too much yield means too much risk. Pass on Milken's universe.
choices.outcome: You stay out of the junk-bond casino while everyone else gorges. Your empire grows slower and survives the 1990 reckoning intact — a quiet heresy against the decade's theology.

events.title: Greed Is Good, {given_name}
events.scene: Wall Street is exploding. The Dow has broken 1,000 and keeps going. Investment bankers are twenty-seven years old and earning two million a year. Midtown office buildings can't be built fast enough to house the new Goldman trading floors. The money sloshing out of Wall Street has to go somewhere — and it is starting to flow straight into luxury Manhattan real estate.
events.researchNote: The 1980s bull market generated enormous Wall Street bonuses that flowed into Manhattan luxury real estate, fueling demand for high-end condominiums and hotels at exactly the moment Trump Tower was opening and Trump was marketing to that demographic.

choices.text: Reposition everything as luxury. The Wall Street bonus class is your new customer.
choices.outcome: You market marble lobbies and gilded fixtures to a generation that measures worth in square feet and gold trim. The Trump brand becomes synonymous with 1980s excess — which is exactly what these buyers want.

choices.text: Bring in Wall Street equity partners to fund expansion at scale.
choices.outcome: Goldman and Bear come to the table. The capital accelerates the empire, but now you have partners who read balance sheets for a living — and they will not be patient when the cycle turns.

choices.text: Stay focused on middle-market deals. The Wall Street crowd is a bubble.
choices.outcome: You miss the luxury wave but avoid its inevitable crash. A contrarian choice that looks wise in 1991 and foolish for all the years in between.

events.title: The Deregulator's Gift
events.scene: Reagan is remaking Washington's relationship with capital: tax cuts, deregulation, a bonfire of rules that once constrained what banks could lend and who they could lend to. For a developer who lives on other people's money, every deregulated dollar is a personal invitation. The question is whether you exploit the new rules, shape them from the inside, or let your rivals drink first.
events.researchNote: Reagan-era deregulation of financial markets, including the Garn-St Germain Depository Institutions Act of 1982, loosened lending restrictions and fueled the credit expansion that enabled the 1980s real estate boom and, eventually, the S&L crisis.

choices.text: Hire lobbyists and shape the deregulation in your favor before the ink dries.
choices.outcome: Your lobbyists get a carve-out in the tax bill that no one notices for a decade. The gift keeps giving long after Reagan is gone.

choices.text: Skip the politics and just borrow every dollar the deregulated banks will lend.
choices.outcome: The spigot opens and you drink. The portfolio doubles in eighteen months. The structural fragility is someone else's problem — probably the banks'.

choices.text: Go on the record cautioning that everyone is borrowing too much.
choices.outcome: The Wall Street Journal runs your quote and calls you a contrarian. Rivals laugh — until they don't. You gain a reputation for foresight that costs you nothing except being right early.

events.title: The Met Gala Problem
events.scene: An invitation arrives — the Metropolitan Museum of Art's Costume Institute benefit, the citadel of old-money social power. The event committee, chaired by women with surnames like Astor and Whitney, has decided to admit some new-money developers this year. Jackie O. sits on the curatorial board. It is simultaneously a coronation and a trial. Every tabloid photographer in Manhattan will be at the door.
events.researchNote: By the mid-1980s Trump and Ivana had become fixtures of the New York social circuit at precisely the inflection point when old-money gatekeepers were grudgingly absorbing new-money developers into elite philanthropy; the Trumps cultivated this world while simultaneously antagonizing it.

choices.text: Attend, be gracious, let Ivana shine, and play the grateful arriviste.
choices.outcome: The society photographers love you both. W magazine does a spread. For one evening the old-money set treats you as one of their own — and you file away exactly which buttons were pressed.

choices.text: Show up with a gold-lamé entourage and a view that the whole gala is theater.
choices.outcome: The dowagers whisper 'gauche.' Every camera lens in the building follows you. By midnight you are the headline — not the host. The old-money crowd's contempt is the best publicity you have ever had.

choices.text: Send a donation and skip it. Their approval is a leash you don't need.
choices.outcome: The no-show is a minor tabloid item. The message is clear: you are not auditioning. Some read defiance; others read insecurity. You do not care which.

choices.text: Offer to underwrite the entire gala in exchange for the Trump name on the program.
choices.outcome: The committee accepts the check and grinds its teeth over the program cover. You have bought the room's tolerance if not its affection — and your name is in every Upper East Side living room by morning.

events.title: Who Made Who
events.scene: A profile in New York magazine is asking the question everyone whispers: how much of the {surname} empire is your father's and how much is {given_name}'s? The piece is fair but precise — it tallies your father's loans, his co-signatures, his political connections, the infrastructure that predates {given_name}'s birth. You can answer it honestly or craft the reply that builds the myth.
events.researchNote: Fred Trump's political connections (especially with Brooklyn Democratic machine figures) and direct financial support were critical to Donald's early deals, including the Commodore and Trump Tower. The 'self-made' narrative was always contested by reporters who tracked the paper trail.

choices.text: Give Fred full credit in the profile. Gratitude is strength, not weakness.
choices.outcome: The profile lands warmly. Fred is proud; the establishment quietly respects the honesty. But the self-made myth can never be fully constructed from this foundation.

choices.text: Tell the reporter you started from nothing. Fred was retired background.
choices.outcome: The myth lands in print and sticks. A generation will believe you built it alone. The reporters who know the truth will keep filing; one day the paper trail wins.

choices.text: Don't play. No comment, no access — let them print what they can prove.
choices.outcome: The profile runs anyway, more skeptical for the silence. The myth remains unbuilt. You sacrifice a chance to write your own story and let others write it for you.

choices.text: Give exclusive access to a rival reporter with a more flattering angle to drown out the first piece.
choices.outcome: Two Trump profiles publish the same week; yours drowns the other. The self-made myth doesn't need to be true — it just needs to be louder.

events.title: Fred's War Secret
events.scene: A lawsuit by a disgruntled former contractor surfaces a deposition in which Fred refers to himself as the son of Swedish immigrants. A researcher from the Times recognizes the discrepancy — the immigration papers say Friedrich Drumpf, Bavaria. Post-war New York's Jewish real estate community made the Trumps' borrowed Swedish identity an economic necessity; now that necessity is a liability.
events.researchNote: Friedrich Trump (born 1869, Kallstadt, Bavaria) immigrated to the U.S. in 1902. Fred Trump's fiction of Swedish ancestry was a strategic cover in a market where anti-German sentiment — raw after two World Wars — could cost a landlord tenants, contractors, and partners in New York's heavily Jewish outer-borough real estate world.

choices.text: Call the Times editor directly and offer a bigger story to kill it.
choices.outcome: The Times editor takes the trade. The researcher is redirected to a story about the garbage strike. The Drumpf question goes back to sleep — for now.

choices.text: Quietly correct the record: acknowledge German heritage, frame Friedrich as a pioneering immigrant entrepreneur.
choices.outcome: The story runs as a human-interest piece about a Bavarian immigrant family. Post-war shadows dissolve; a new narrative about the American Dream takes their place.

choices.text: Have Roy Cohn send a cease-and-desist to the Times.
choices.outcome: Cohn's letter lands on the legal desk and the story dies — temporarily. But 'Trump sues Times over ancestry story' becomes its own item. You have traded one problem for a louder one.

events.title: Staring Down the Bankers
events.scene: Your debt load is staggering and a few projects are bleeding cash. The bankers grow nervous, but you have learned a Cohn-grade truth: if you owe a bank a million, they own you; if you owe them a billion, you own them. You can negotiate from weakness or from manufactured strength.
events.researchNote: By the late 1980s Trump's empire carried enormous bank and bond debt; when the reckoning came around 1990, his banks chose to restructure rather than foreclose, illustrating the leverage a borrower can wield when the debts are large enough to threaten the lenders.

choices.text: Bluff the banks: too big to let fail.
choices.outcome: You dare the bankers to pull the plug and watch their own balance sheets explode. The bluff buys time, but the debt only grows hungrier.

choices.text: Open the books and restructure in good faith.
choices.outcome: You sit down with the lenders, lay it bare, and carve out a survivable plan. Humbling, but the empire might just outlast the decade.

choices.text: Sell the weakest assets to slash the debt now.
choices.outcome: You amputate the losers to save the body. The legend shrinks, but the bankruptcy wolf is driven back from the door.

events.title: The Outer-Borough Exodus
events.scene: Your father’s empire of brick apartments in Queens and Brooklyn is stable, boring, and suffocatingly provincial. While your brother Fred Jr. drinks and dreams of escaping to the clouds as a commercial pilot, you look across the East River toward the jagged skyline of Manhattan, where the real money and glory reside. You must convince the old man that the family's future lies in luxury, not FHA-backed laundry rooms.
events.researchNote: Donald Trump's early career was defined by his desire to escape his father Fred's outer-borough rental empire and build in Manhattan.

choices.text: Support Fred Jr.'s flying dreams to clear your path to the family crown.
choices.outcome: With Fred Jr. dismissed as a simple flyer, your father begins to look at you as the sole heir capable of carrying the family name.

choices.text: Demean Fred Jr.'s 'bus driver in the sky' ambitions to please your father.
choices.outcome: You win your father's cold approval by echoing his contempt, leaving your brother isolated and broken.

choices.text: Demand your own small stake in Queens to leverage into Manhattan seed money.
choices.outcome: You wring a share of the rental profits from the old man, immediately earmarking it for Manhattan options.

events.title: The Department of Justice Comes Knocking
events.scene: The Nixon administration's Department of Justice has sued your family firm, accusing you of coding rental applications to systematically reject Black applicants. Your father wants to settle quietly, but a ferocious young lawyer named Roy Cohn advises a scorched-earth counter-suit for $100 million.
events.researchNote: In 1973, the DOJ sued the Trumps for violating the Fair Housing Act; Roy Cohn represented them and launched a massive counter-suit.

choices.text: Hire Roy Cohn to countersue and launch a vicious public offensive.
choices.outcome: You call a press conference to accuse the government of Gestapo tactics. The establishment is shocked, but you have found your mentor.

choices.text: Settle quietly, promising integration while quietly hardening screening procedures.
choices.outcome: The headlines fade quickly. You avoid a public trial, but the family's name bears a quiet, indelible stain.

choices.text: Blame the rental agents and implement a token minority-hiring program.
choices.outcome: Your field agents take the fall. You appease the regulators with empty handshakes and press releases.

events.title: The Grand Hyatt Gamble
events.scene: The old Commodore Hotel next to Grand Central is a decaying, bankrupt eyesore. Manhattan is near bankruptcy, but you see an opportunity: buy the hotel, partner with Hyatt, and demand a historically unprecedented 40-year tax abatement from a desperate city hall.
events.researchNote: Trump partnered with Hyatt in 1976 to renovate the Commodore Hotel, securing a massive 40-year tax abatement from New York City.

choices.text: Threaten to walk unless the city grants the full 40-year tax break.
choices.outcome: They fold. The city grants the forty-year abatement, effectively subsidizing your entry into Manhattan's elite.

choices.text: Use your father's political connections to grease the wheels of the Board of Estimate.
choices.outcome: Quiet dinners with Brooklyn machine politicians do the trick, though the old man reminds you who really built your bridge to Manhattan.

choices.text: Offer a minor profit-sharing deal to the city to secure quicker approval.
choices.outcome: The press praises your civic-mindedness, but Wall Street whispers that you left too much money on the table.

events.title: The Shadow on the Line
events.scene: Your older brother, Fred Jr., has succumbed entirely to alcoholism, his pilot career ruined, living back in your parents' attic. Your father views him as a pathetic failure; you must decide how to handle the brother who should have been the heir.
events.researchNote: Fred Trump Jr.'s struggle with alcoholism and his tragic decline deeply shaped Donald Trump's lifelong aversion to alcohol.

choices.text: Cut him off entirely to keep his messy dependencies away from the family assets.
choices.outcome: He is excised from the ledger. You keep the estate safe, but the cold silence in the family home is deafening.

choices.text: Offer him a low-level, sober job in the Queens leasing office.
choices.outcome: He takes the job, a shadow of the handsome pilot he once was, surviving on family charity.

choices.text: Lecture him brutally on willpower, using his decline as your own lesson in discipline.
choices.outcome: He shrinks from your anger. You swear never to touch a drop of liquor, hardening your own resolve as his fades.

events.title: The Bas-Reliefs of Bonwit Teller
events.scene: To clear the path for your gleaming Fifth Avenue tower, you must demolish the old Bonwit Teller building. Prominent art curators have begged you to preserve the building's historic Art Deco limestone reliefs. Jacking them down carefully will delay construction and cost thousands.
events.researchNote: In 1980, Trump demolished the Bonwit Teller building, destroying its famous Art Deco reliefs despite promises to donate them to the Met, sparking outrage.

choices.text: Order the workers to smash the reliefs under cover of night to save time and money.
choices.outcome: Jackhammers reduce the priceless limestone to dust. The New York Times is apoplectic, but your construction timeline is secure.

choices.text: Announce they are 'worthless' and unsafe to salvage, using an alias to berate journalists.
choices.outcome: Calling reporters as 'John Barron,' you insist the stones were junk anyway. The press is confused; your associates are amused.

choices.text: Pay the premium to salvage them, buying social credit with the Metropolitan Museum.
choices.outcome: The old-money boards offer polite applause, though your accountants weep at the unnecessary delay.

events.title: The Wollman Rink Challenge
events.scene: For six years, the city of New York has bungled the renovation of the Wollman Skating Rink in Central Park, spending millions to produce nothing but leaking pipes. You publicly mock the Mayor and offer to finish the job yourself in six months, for free, if the city lets you run it.
events.researchNote: Trump successfully took over and completed the Wollman Rink renovation in 1986 under budget and ahead of schedule to mock the city's incompetence.

choices.text: Leverage the project's rapid completion into a massive PR blitz against city bureaucracy.
choices.outcome: You deliver the rink ahead of schedule. The public loves the swaggering developer who can get things done while city hall fumbles.

choices.text: Use union-free, cut-rate contractors to ensure it finishes under budget.
choices.outcome: The labor leaders are furious, but the speed of completion vindicates your brutal, cost-cutting methods.

choices.text: Keep the rink's naming rights in perpetuity, turning a civic duty into a permanent billboard.
choices.outcome: Every child skating on the ice looks up at your name in bold letters, burned into the park's landscape.

events.title: The Conquest of Palm Beach
events.scene: Mar-a-Lago, the 126-room grand estate of the late Marjorie Merriweather Post, is the crown jewel of Palm Beach. The snobbish old-money locals do not want a loud, outer-borough builder in their secluded paradise. When they reject your initial bid, you buy the beachfront land directly in front of the estate and threaten to block their view.
events.researchNote: Trump bought the beachfront land in front of Mar-a-Lago to devalue the property and force the sellers to accept his low bid of around $10 million in 1985.

choices.text: Threaten to build the 'world's ugliest home' to force their hand, then buy it cheap.
choices.outcome: The foundation panic-sells. You acquire the palace for a song, cementing your reputation as a ruthless outsider.

choices.text: Pay the full asking price to win the approval of the Palm Beach social register.
choices.outcome: You are admitted to the parties, but the old-money elites still whisper about your gold-plated bathroom fixtures behind your back.

choices.text: Turn the estate into a private, highly commercial club immediately to spite the locals.
choices.outcome: By opening the doors to anyone who can write a check, you destroy the exclusivity the town council spent decades defending.

events.title: The Ironclad Nuptials
events.scene: Your wife, the Czech-born model and interior designer you put in charge of your Atlantic City castles, is demanding a renegotiated postnuptial agreement. She knows the empire runs on her labor and image as much as yours. You must protect the dynasty's assets without causing a public, brand-damaging divorce.
events.researchNote: Donald and Ivana Trump’s marriage was defined by multiple renegotiated prenuptial and postnuptial agreements as her role in the company grew.

choices.text: Force her to sign an aggressive, cash-limited agreement using your legal team.
choices.outcome: She signs, but the warmth leaves your home. The partnership is now purely transactional, recorded in billable hours.

choices.text: Give her a substantial cut of the real estate holdings to preserve the corporate alliance.
choices.outcome: The joint brand remains intact. She continues to design the gold-leaf lobbies that sell your shared dream to the masses.

choices.text: Offer her the presidency of the Plaza Hotel instead of hard equity.
choices.outcome: She accepts the glittering title and works herself to exhaustion at the Plaza, keeping her too busy to audit your bank accounts.
